claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is primarily an artifact of systematic scribal abbreviation, where common root words are truncated with consistent suffixes, making truncated forms appear unique when they share stems with high-frequency words.
evidence_for: Hapax ratio of 70.1% is anomalously high compared to natural languages (~40-50%)
Top frequent words like 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy' suggest suffix-sharing morphology
Average word length of 5.03 is short, consistent with abbreviation practices
The moderate Zipf exponent (0.8946) diverges from natural language ~1.0, consistent with vocabulary inflation from abbreviation artifacts
evidence_against: If abbreviation caused hapax inflation, stemming should recover a lower effective vocabulary â€” no such reduction has been confirmed
Glyph entropy (3.8627) is close to natural language values, not what pure abbreviation noise would produce
The Currier A/B split shows systematic regional differences inconsistent with purely scribal abbreviation
test: Cluster all 8,261 unique words by shared n-grams (n=2,3 suffix/prefix), then measure what fraction of hapax words share a suffix of length â‰¥2 with a high-frequency word (top 500). Compare resulting stem-collapsed vocabulary size to expected hapax ratio for natural language.
test_metric: Post-stemming hapax ratio and stem-cluster coverage of hapax words
test_threshold: If >60% of hapax words cluster within 1-2 glyph edits of a high-frequency word, abbreviation hypothesis is supported; if post-stemming hapax ratio drops below 45%, strongly supported

tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: Distinguishing genuine vocabulary diversity from scribal abbreviation artifact is critical: if the hapax inflation is artifactual, the effective vocabulary is much smaller, dramatically narrowing candidate cipher and language hypotheses
